1: Psalm 15 is an entrance liturgy — a question-and-answer ritual performed at the gates of the sanctuary before entry. Psalm 24 has the same structure ("Who may ascend the hill of the LORD?"), and Isaiah 33:14-16 and Micah 6:6-8 show the same genre operating in prophetic contexts. Pilgrims approaching the sanctuary would ask "Who may dwell in your sanctuary?" and a priest or the congregation would answer with the ethical requirements. The two verbs — gûr (sojourn, temporary dwelling) and šākan (dwell, permanent residence) — together span both temporary access and full covenant belonging to the holy mountain.
2: "The one who walks blamelessly (tāmîm), does what is right (ṣedeq), and speaks truth from the heart" — three positive qualifications. Tāmîm (blameless, integrity of wholeness) is the term used of Noah (Genesis 6:9) and Job (Job 1:1) — not sinless perfection but undivided moral integrity; the opposite of the divided heart in Psalms 12:2. "Does what is right" is covenant-justice in practice. "Speaks truth from the heart" — heart and mouth aligned — is the antithesis of the duplicity condemned in Ps 12 and the flattery condemned throughout the Psalter.
3: "Who does not slander with the tongue (rāḡal ʿal-lĕšônô)" — literally "foot it on his tongue," the idiom for one who goes about from house to house bearing tales (cf. Leviticus 19:16 — "do not go about spreading slander"). Social harm (rāʿāh) to a neighbor and reproach against a friend cover the range of speech that damages reputation. In ancient Israel, one's standing in the community was inseparable from one's social life — to damage reputation was to harm the person at the most fundamental level of their social existence.
4: "Who despises the contemptible but honors those who fear the LORD; who keeps an oath even when it costs (nišbaʿ lĕhāraʿ wĕlōʾ yāmîr)" — to honor YHWH-fearers while despising the dishonorable inverts normal social dynamics where power and wealth determine honor. "Swears to his own hurt and does not change" — in ancient commercial and social life, oaths were legally and spiritually binding; to renege on a sworn commitment was both social treachery and covenant violation. The one who keeps costly oaths demonstrates that their word is genuinely reliable, not self-serving.
5: "Who does not lend money at interest (nešek) or take a bribe (šōḥad) against the innocent" — the Torah prohibited lending at interest to fellow Israelites (Exodus 22:25; Leviticus 25:35-37; Deuteronomy 23:19-20) though permitted it with foreigners. Interest-bearing loans were a primary mechanism of predatory wealth accumulation in the ancient economy: the small farmer who borrowed after a crop failure could lose his land within a season (cf. Nehemiah 5:1-5). The bribe against the innocent was the standard form of judicial corruption that perverted the court system for the wealthy (Exodus 23:8; Deuteronomy 16:19; Amos 5:12). "Whoever does these things will never be moved (lōʾ yimmôṭ lĕʿôlām)" — covenant stability.
